segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a vibrant display of polished crystals and gemstones, including amethyst and rose quartz, arranged on a market stall with visible price tags, as a calm, serious female narrator states, "For a long time, they were just stones. But those days are gone." Ethereal, slightly ominous music plays as the scene transitions to a woman with her hair in a bun, wearing a floral blouse, gently touching a pink crystal to her neck. An upbeat, commercial-sounding female voice explains that a "gua sha stone" can be used not only as an anti-aging tool but also to do something good for your skin and relax your facial muscles. The narrator adds that some crystals are supposed to help against wrinkles, while others are for happiness, as delicate crystal jewelry with gold accents is shown laid out on a white surface. The narrative continues with the "magic of different crystals and spirituality," showing a woman with long blonde hair in a patterned sweater in a cozy room, followed by a laptop screen displaying a woman with bright green hair and a crystal necklace. A woman with a German accent then states, "I really believe in it." The narrator observes that "Minerals and crystals have become big business, but looking behind the scenes is difficult," as a woman with short brown hair types on a laptop in a home office. The scene shifts to an outdoor, arid landscape where a man in a blue shirt walks through tall dry grass, followed by a group of people examining rocks. A male voice with a German accent states, "We hope you understand, but we do not comment on our suppliers." Another male voice with a German accent declares, "It's not okay. It shouldn't be okay." The narrator then poses the critical questions, "Who pays? And at what price?" as a person uses a pickaxe to dig into the earth, accompanied by the distinct sound of the pickaxe hitting rock. The segment concludes with the title 'THE DIRTY BUSINESS OF BEAUTY' and credits for 'A Film by NADJA MITZKAT ZITA ZENGERLING', reinforcing the documentary’s critical theme.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute begins with an upbeat, slightly mysterious background music as a bustling train station scene unfolds, showing a woman in a dark coat and backpack descending a staircase amidst commuters and a train, establishing a fast-paced urban environment. A female voiceover explains, "We're on our way to Hanover to find out more about this trend that's become so popular on social media." The scene quickly transitions to close-up beauty tutorials, accompanied by a pop/R&B music track with a "Yeah" vocal sample. A woman applies a product with a small brush, followed by a woman with long dark hair and large hoop earrings holding a pink heart-shaped rose quartz facial sculptor. Another woman with long blonde hair and a white sweater uses a similar tool, while a female voice (the Woman with long brown hair and headband) shares, "I personally love crystals. I feel like they hold a very, very, very beautiful energy." The initial female voiceover returns, stating, "Influencers show us how to use facial rollers and gua sha stones," as a woman with short brown hair and tattoos demonstrates a dual-ended facial roller. A woman with long brown hair and a gold watch then uses a rose quartz facial roller on her forehead, guided by on-screen text 'FOREHEAD TO EAR' and a tutorial voice instructing, "And then out to the side." This voice continues, "Roll over the area under your eyes and get that lymphatic fluid moving," with a German voice in the background reiterating the process. The tutorial voice specifies, "From your eyelid to your ear. Seven times per side." The woman with long brown hair and a floral headband, smiling with text overlays 'Just 5 Minutes a day!' and '@rosental_organics', confidently adds, "And then you won't need anti-aging creams anymore." The scene transitions to a more stylized, fashion-focused setting with dramatic music as the initial female voiceover announces, "And even stars are talking about these stones." Title cards like 'IN THE BAG with Claudia Schiffer' appear, showing a woman with red hair in a vibrant red chair with a black handbag, and a woman with blonde hair in a patterned sweater with a leather handbag. The Woman with blonde hair introduces herself as Claudia Schiffer, saying, "Hi Vogue, I'm Claudia Schiffer," followed by a male voice stating, "I'm Bill," and another female voice, "I'm Bad Mom's J." Claudia Schiffer then declares, "And today I'm gonna show you what's in my bag." The initial female voiceover concludes, "And reveal their favorites in stylized clips," as the woman with long blonde hair, seated in a bright room with a scenic view, pulls a small item from her bag, all set to an upbeat and cinematic musical score.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens at a bustling gemstone fair, where a vendor woman with dark hair, wearing a white shirt and light gray vest, stands behind a glass display case filled with jewelry and crystals, including amethyst and rose quartz. She speaks directly to the camera in English with a slight accent, explaining that 'the classics like rose quartz, amethyst, or the famous gypsum selenite' and 'green aventurine' were completely sold out last year, expressing how 'baffled' they were. The display is clearly labeled 'Münchner Heilsteine Edelsteine-Schmuck.' The scene then shifts to an interaction at the fair, where a woman with long dark hair and glasses holds a phone displaying a TikTok video about crystals, while a woman with long blonde hair in a beige trench coat holds a wallet. A female voice asks, 'If you have time, could you show me on TikTok?' This leads into a series of TikTok clips, accompanied by upbeat, pop-like music with a female vocal sample. One clip features a close-up of rose quartz with text overlays like 'Love and self love,' 'Harmony,' and 'Trust.' Another shows a bowl of crystals for anger, and a hand holding rose quartz with text 'Four reasons why you can only love rose quartz,' followed by a woman with short blonde hair holding a crystal. A voiceover explains that 'people who talk about their benefits and the best way to use them' are featured, and another voice asks, 'Do you know rose quartz?' before declaring, 'Rose quartz is the stone for everything to do with love.' A male voiceover adds that 'these crystals are touted as esoteric marvels' in posts making 'big promises.' The final clip shows a woman with bright green hair, wearing a crystal necklace, speaking on a laptop screen, representing a social media influencer. The vendor's voice returns, stating that 'gemstones with healing properties are trendy right now,' and reiterating, 'This is rose quartz. It's a really wonderful crystal,' as the music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens at a bustling gemstone fair, where a man in a white shirt with a patterned collar speaks animatedly in German (translated), gesturing as he explains the harsh realities of the mining industry: the poor conditions, lack of education, and how people, even children as young as 14, are forced to haul stones as their profession. He engages with a woman wearing a face mask, who asks in English if this constitutes 'developmental support,' to which he responds that it's support within the framework of their culture, hoping for social system development. The camera pulls back to reveal the lively indoor market with numerous stalls displaying polished crystals and jewelry, including amethyst and rose quartz, with visible price tags and signs like 'Schmuck & Stein' and 'Münchner Heilsteine Edelsteine-Schmuck'. A gentle, melancholic background music begins as an English-speaking narrator interjects, stating that little support usually reaches the global South, which primarily serves as cheap raw material suppliers, and that miners often work under dangerous, long-standing problematic conditions. The scene abruptly cuts to a 2015 archive news segment, where a German-speaking news anchor (translated) reports on a 'Tragedy at Jade Mine' in Myanmar, detailing a landslide that killed over 90 people, with many more missing, and highlighting the lucrative nature of the jade industry. Footage of rescue workers in a rocky, debris-filled area and a person with a pickaxe underscores the dangerous extraction process, starkly contrasting the commercial beauty of the gemstones with their 'dirty business' origins.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with sunlit archive footage from 2007, showing a wide mining operation where a yellow dump truck unloads dark material into a pit, surrounded by a large group of people in casual clothing moving through rocky terrain. A male voice, initially speaking German then English, states that 'hardly any of it reaches the people living here.' The scene shifts to a closer view of miners in a dusty, open-pit mine, using shovels and pickaxes, with one man in a knitted hat and another in a striped shirt, as the male narrator describes 'dusty hot gold mines in Niger,' where 'only children can fit in the narrow shafts,' and that 'hunger... drives most families here, forcing them to risk their lives every day in the illegal mines.' The footage then transitions to archive material from 2022, showing a young boy in a dark shirt and patterned pants digging in reddish-brown earth with a small tool, placing soil into a bowl, later assisted by a young girl in a red top. A female narrator's voice begins, explaining, 'We can't find much on rose quartz mines in Madagascar, except for an article in the British newspaper The Guardian about the mining,' adding that 'the mining conditions are devastating.' She continues, stating that 'based on export volumes, Madagascar is one of the world's top three export nations of precious and semi-precious stones.' The scene abruptly changes to a modern urban setting, where a woman with short brown hair, wearing a light pink t-shirt and jeans, sits at a desk with a computer, reading an article titled 'Dark crystals: the brutal reality behind a booming wellness craze' on a laptop screen. The camera zooms in on her eye, then shows her hand using a computer mouse, which transitions into a digital world map. The map highlights countries in Africa and South America, with labels appearing sequentially: Mozambique, Brazil, Madagascar, South Africa, and Uruguay, indicating major sources of crystal mining, with data sourced from the UN Comtrade Database (2020). The female narrator concludes that Madagascar 'sells quantities almost as large as export giant Brazil,' before her speech is cut off.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a digital world map highlighting Mozambique, Brazil, Madagascar, South Africa, and Uruguay, with Madagascar emphasized in yellow as a major crystal source based on 2020 UN Comtrade Database numbers. A female narrator questions how such a small country exports so much and asks if stones from Madagascar end up in Germany, explaining they asked German companies about their rose quartz suppliers. The scene then transitions to a woman with short brown hair, wearing a gray sweater, typing on a black ASUS laptop at a wooden desk. Her voice is heard saying "Vielen Dank für Ihre...", followed by a male voice stating that companies do not comment on their suppliers. The woman is then seen smiling while holding a smartphone, and her voice expresses "Great" and later, "I hate these hotlines." She continues to speak, noting that their messages to companies "didn't really bear fruit" as they were "tight-lipped or didn't respond at all." The video then cuts to a close-up of a German document from the Embassy of the Republic of Madagascar in Germany, titled 'Visum für Film, Reportage und Dokumentarfilm drehs,' detailing strict visa requirements for film and documentary work, including prior approval from local authorities. Throughout, background music plays, and the female narrator concludes by stating their decision to investigate in Madagascar, acknowledging it's "not so easy," followed by a brief male vocalization.

The setting then shifts to the exterior of a modern restaurant with a stone facade and glass doors, where a security guard in a dark uniform stands near the entrance. Inside, the camera captures a calm and professional journalistic interview taking place in a contemporary, well-lit space featuring wooden tables, decorative plants, and large windows. A woman, likely Valerie, with her hair tied back and wearing a light gray denim jacket with a floral skirt, is seated at a wooden table, holding a notepad and pen, with a walkie-talkie clipped to her hip, actively taking notes and gesturing as she speaks. Across from her, a man with short curly black hair and a mustache, wearing a brown zip-up jacket, listens attentively with a small microphone clipped to his jacket. The narrator explains that "He tells us the miners' situation is a most urgent problem." The man then speaks with a clear, calm male voice and a non-native English accent, stating that "The number of miners are increasing every year," estimating "up to 500,000 people that are working in the mining sectors." He continues, expressing concern that "we don't know, we don't have a full detail about their numbers and or their identity," highlighting this as "one of the problem" as "they're just flocking from here to here and moving from one location to another." The camera alternates between close-ups of the man speaking and wider shots showing the restaurant’s interior, which also features a vase with white flowers on the table. The female narrator concludes by stating, "We learn from Valerie that currently most..." as the segment ends.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a bright, modern restaurant, where a woman with short brown hair in a light gray denim jacket, holding a notepad and pen with a walkie-talkie clipped to her hip, is engaged in a serious conversation across a wooden table with a man in a beige sweater. A female narrator's clear English voice immediately sets the context, explaining that much of the mining in Madagascar occurs without permits, with 80% of mines being makeshift and artisanal, relying on human labor. The camera alternates between over-the-shoulder shots and close-ups, capturing their interaction amidst indistinct chatter. The scene then widens to reveal a woman in a blue vest with 'REGULATION DES MEDIAS' on the back, standing beside the man, as the narrator expresses concern about their state agent chaperone, later identified as Olivia, potentially causing problems for workers, especially after she questioned someone named Valérie and took notes. The woman in the denim jacket then turns to speak directly to the camera, her expression earnest and focused, and is heard speaking in German, expressing that it's a "very strange situation" and that she's "a bit stressed" because she told the man they wanted to visit the mines, and Olivia is now questioning him about what he told them. The narrator then confirms that "Luckily, Olivia doesn't hear about those plans." To be on the safe side, the narrator explains, instead of going to the mines, they decide to head to the coastal town of Toamasina. The video transitions to an outdoor urban scene in Madagascar, showing a bustling cobblestone street with vendors, a white taxi, and colorful buildings, while a map graphic highlights Antananarivo and Toamasina. The segment concludes with the woman in the denim jacket arriving at the Hotel Neptune, pulling a suitcase, as a yellow tuk-tuk passes by, signaling the start of her journey in Madagascar, accompanied by the start of upbeat, rhythmic world music, as the narrator describes Toamasina as the most important trading port.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a woman in a striped shirt and orange safety vest, accompanied by a man in a hat and vest, exiting a white van to enter the Port de Toamasina. As they proceed through the port's entrance, marked by a 'PORT DE TOAMASINA' sign, and pass a security checkpoint, the woman's voice narrates their mission: to track rose quartz from the mines to the port and then worldwide, explaining they needed three permits to film. She recounts how they submitted applications before their trip, but the port director rejected a verbal agreement from customs, forcing them to leave the premises. The scene transitions to an exterior view of the bustling port with a large cargo ship, cranes, and containers, as the narrator expresses her annoyance at being denied access to observe rose quartz loading and interview workers, despite initially being told they had all the necessary permits. A brief male interjection of "Yeah" is heard, followed by the woman asking for clarification. Later, on a sandy beach near the port, the woman, now without her vest, speaks to the camera with the port infrastructure behind her, further lamenting that even "Olivia," their supervisor from the Ministry of Communications, couldn't help. She then walks along the beach past a local settlement, concluding with a shot of children playing with blue barrels in a paved area, highlighting the local environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a flooded street in a Madagascar town, with murky water covering the pavement, as a calm female English voice states, "And that wasn't all." The scene transitions to an indoor interview with a woman with short brown hair, wearing a striped shirt, who speaks expressively while gesturing with her hands. During this, a German female voice is heard briefly, followed by the English narrator explaining that someone tested positive for COVID, making the situation "even more absurd" as "she started coughing two or three days ago." A stylized map graphic of Madagascar then appears, highlighting Antananarivo, Toamasina, and Antsirabé with orange markers, as the narrator continues, "Fortunately, we were still testing negative, so we continued on our journey heading to Ancirabé." Traditional, slightly melancholic string music begins to play in the background. The video cuts to a bustling street scene in a local town, showing people walking past small shops and a vendor selling snacks, followed by a close-up of a hand holding a bag of roasted peanuts. The narrator adds, "Olivia had to go into quarantine, meaning we were able to go to the Rose Quartz mines without her." The scene shifts to two men playing foosball, then a man in a red jacket walking past a shop with a glass display, and another man carrying a large sack, while the narrator explains, "Our base was in Ancirabé, the largest city in the region." The traditional music continues. Finally, a woman with a red scarf enters a modest room with a bed and desk, turns to face the camera, and speaks directly. Her voice, distinct from the earlier narrator, explains, "Travel times in Madagascar tend to be long, so we set off early in the morning." The sound of a vehicle engine is heard, and she continues, sounding tired, "It's around 5 AM. I'm still really tired. We're going to the mines now. It's a three-hour drive plus..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a woman with short brown hair, wearing a red scarf and a light-colored jacket, standing in a modestly furnished room with warm lighting. She turns to face the camera with a serious expression, speaking in German and then English, stating, "Let's see what awaits us. In any case, things are tense." Light, investigative background music begins as the scene transitions to a vehicle’s side mirror reflecting a sunset over a hilly landscape, indicating the start of a journey. A distinct female narrator's voice explains that "According to the World Bank, about 80% of Madagascar's population lives below the international poverty line," and that "Rural areas in particular are poor," with jobs primarily in agriculture or mining. The perspective shifts to the interior of a moving vehicle, showing a dusty, rural road flanked by dry grass and sparse trees under a bright sky, accompanied by the sounds of the vehicle's engine and road noise. The narrator continues, noting, "To get to one of these mines, we have to leave the paved road." The camera captures the driver’s side view, revealing a small, simple house with a thatched roof and a dirt yard. As the vehicle continues, it approaches a river with a concrete bridge, and the driver, the same woman with short brown hair now wearing a white sleeveless top, is seen steering. The rearview mirror shows a small black figurine hanging. The vehicle drives along a dirt path near the river, passing a group of children playing in the background. The woman turns to the camera again, speaking directly in German and then English, explaining, "We just came to a mine that we know is run by one family, but we can't go to the mine itself, it's closed. There was an accident and someone was seriously injured." The vehicle then moves through a dry, open landscape with scattered trees and distant hills, eventually stopping near a group of people, including a man in a red shirt and white hat, who appears to be waving or gesturing. The narrator's voice returns, stating, "Our search continues. Our interpreter helps us ask questions as we look for a mine that's still in operation." The final shot shows the woman in the driver’s seat, looking out the window at a vast, sunlit field, suggesting a moment of reflection or observation, as the music and vehicle sounds continue.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a dry, grassy landscape under a bright blue sky, accompanied by light, adventurous background music and indistinct chatter. A group of people, including a woman with short brown hair, a white t-shirt, and a dark blue backpack, walks along a dusty path, their footsteps audible on the gravel. The woman narrates, stating they've "just received the location of a mine, and are on our way." She then lightheartedly remarks, "I look like I'm on safari." As the scene shifts to a panoramic view of a dry valley, another woman's voice exclaims, "And it's great," followed by a man playfully commenting on the woman's "Jack Wolfskin backpack." The main woman responds, "My grandma gave it to me." She then speaks directly to the camera with an earnest expression, expressing uncertainty about how people will react to them, before explaining they are "almost there now, just 10 more minutes on foot." She adds that they plan to "first turn off the camera" to meet people without it. The scene transitions to a rocky area where a group of local people, including children, are gathered. A man's brief German exclamation is heard. The woman clarifies that the locals are "already expecting us" and "don't mind us visiting, and we can keep filming." The focus shifts to one person carefully handling a large, translucent crystal with a hammer, with the distinct sound of the hammer hitting rock. The woman begins to explain that "Rose quartz deposits exist in various places in Madagascar," and here, "it's under just a thin layer of soil. Freshly mined, the stones can be extremely..." before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a young boy, later identified as 15-year-old Naina, in a dark shirt, bent over and working in a dry, rocky field under bright sunlight. A female narrator explains that this is one of the reasons why working in mines is considered one of the worst forms of child labor, and why labor laws in Madagascar prohibit anyone under the age of 18 from working in them. The scene quickly expands to reveal a group of local people, including children, engaged in mining or collecting large, translucent crystals from the ground, with sounds of movement and clinking stones accompanying their labor. The narrator notes that "these two boys" (referring to Naina and another) stop when they see the crew filming, but later get back to work, carrying the heavy stones. Some workers use hammers to break rocks, while others, like a young boy in a yellow shirt and another in a red shirt, carry heavy sacks or buckets filled with crystals on their heads or shoulders through the rugged, arid terrain. A woman with short brown hair, wearing a white t-shirt and a dark blue backpack, part of the documentary crew, is seen crouching among the group, observing and interacting, even smiling at the camera. Naina, speaking in a non-English language translated by the narrator, shares that his parents also work here, he is the eldest of four siblings, and they came because they don't have enough money. The children, including a girl in a blue shirt carrying a yellow bucket on her head, move with determination despite the physical strain under the harsh sun, which casts strong shadows across the dusty, rocky landscape. The narrator concludes by asking Naina what he would like to change, if possible, highlighting the human cost behind the extraction of these crystals.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a dry, rocky, sunlit crystal mining field, where a group of local people, including men, women, and children, are engaged in manual labor. Sounds of digging, scraping, and indistinct chatter fill the air as men handle large, translucent crystals, some loading them onto a wooden cart. Children are actively involved; a young boy in a blue shirt and red shorts, and a girl in a floral skirt, are seen, with one child carrying a yellow bucket on her head and another a sack of crystals. A young boy in a red and white jacket sits on a yellow jerry can, sorting small crystals. The arid environment, with sparse vegetation and scattered rocks, is highlighted by bright sunlight, and a black cow grazes in the background. The camera pans across the group, capturing close-ups of children’s faces, some looking curiously or wearily at the camera. A narrator explains that in Madagascar, only 63% of children finish primary school, and in 2018, one-fifth of 15 to 24-year-olds couldn't read or write. Amidst the sounds of work, a child's voice expresses a poignant desire to become a doctor, a stark contrast to the narrator's revelation that many parents cannot afford school fees, leading them to bring their children to work. A man identified as Sis, a 20-year-old with short curly black hair and a mustache, wearing a beige sweater over a white collared shirt, sits among the crystals, speaking about feeling tired from work. The narrator then mentions discussing the two younger boys working there with Sis, who states that child labor is "absolutely forbidden," underscoring the harsh reality of daily toil for both adults and children in this crystal extraction site.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a young man in a dusty beige t-shirt with 'PARAMOUNT' on the back, speaking expressively in a local language, translated to reveal his poignant story of dropping out of school at 16 to support his parents because life was unaffordable, stating, "It's not okay. It shouldn't be okay." The setting is a dry, sunlit, rocky mining landscape. The camera pulls back to reveal a group, including a woman with short brown hair in a white t-shirt and light blue headscarf, part of a documentary crew, who comments in English, "He's experienced a similar fate." She then hands the young man a small, polished rose quartz facial roller with a gold handle, introducing it as being from Germany and asking, "Have you ever seen something like this?" The young man, examining the object with curiosity and delight, identifies it as quartz and explains how it's polished by machines abroad, contrasting it with the raw material they mine locally. A man in a blue shirt, crouching nearby, holds a white cloth, while another man's voice, likely his, asks, "Is that gold or anything?" as the roller is passed around a growing group of local workers, including a man in a black 'SUPREME' t-shirt, all showing shared interest and smiles. The warm, collaborative atmosphere highlights a moment of cultural exchange, contrasting the raw mining environment with the refined product, all set against the backdrop of dry grass, scattered rocks, and distant hills under a clear blue sky.

The camera pulls back to reveal a group of local men and boys gathered in a dry, sunlit field, some sitting on rocks, others standing, all focused on the object. The woman with short brown hair, wearing a white t-shirt and light blue headscarf, crouches among them, handing the roller to the young man in the beige t-shirt, identified as 'PARAMOUNT' on his shirt. He smiles broadly, turning the roller over in his hands, while a voice exclaims, "It's amazing!" amidst laughter and excited chatter from the group, including a man in a red shirt and bucket hat and another in a black 'SUPREME' shirt. The woman explains, "It's like a facial roller, and you have to go like this," demonstrating its use. A narrator notes that the laborers seem amused, but the mood changes when the woman reveals the product's European market price, stating, "Sometimes they sell them for like 40 euros." This elicits a shocked "40 euros!" from a local man, and the young man in the beige t-shirt expresses his surprise, saying, "I'm rather shocked that it's so expensive. The narrator explains that "the bigger the pieces the men mine, the better," adding that they are paid "400 per kilo, equivalent to around 10..." highlighting the physical labor involved in crystal extraction and the stark contrast between the raw material and its polished, high-value end product.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a wooden cart loaded with large, translucent crystals, being pulled by a donkey through a dry, rocky landscape under bright sunlight, as a female narrator contextualizes the scene by stating that the value of the crystals is equivalent to "the price of two cigarettes in the village here." The scene transitions to a wide shot of a rural hillside where a man and a child walk along a dirt path, with sparse vegetation and rocky terrain, accompanied by background sounds of children's chatter and distant voices. The camera then moves into a village with thatched-roof huts and children playing barefoot on dusty paths, capturing daily life in a modest, rural community. A motorbike speeds down a cracked asphalt road, kicking up dust, while children run and play near a log with chickens, adding movement and life to the village scene. The narrator explains that "many of the laborers live in the village with their families," and that "nearly half of children in Madagascar between the ages of 5 and 17 have to work." The camera follows a man walking down a dirt road lined with simple houses, some with corrugated metal roofs, under a clear blue sky, before the narrator introduces "Toof from the mine," explaining he "used to work in a gold mine" and "now, it's rose quartz." The scene shifts indoors to a dimly lit room with a bed, a small table, and a red curtain, where a man is seen lying down, as the narrator states Toof "shows us his home where he lives with his wife and one-year-old daughter." Toof, speaking in his local language, identifies the room as "our house" and then "the bedroom" (translated from "Hai fana" and "A fatori"). The camera pans to a traditional cooking area with a stone hearth, metal pots, and ash, which Toof describes as "where we cook and store things" (translated from "A tu fanto" and "Kiteri sambugat"). The man, Toof, exits the room and stands outside, smiling and speaking to the camera, with a thatched hut and greenery in the background, conveying a sense of openness and connection to the environment, as he shares in his local language, translated to English, "When I started working, I began to lose weight because I had to provide for my family." The lighting is natural and warm, emphasizing the rural, sunlit setting throughout the sequence, accompanied by continuous ambient village sounds.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a wide shot of a rural village, where children and adults are seen playing and standing near a simple white house with a corrugated metal roof on a dusty red-earth yard, as a female narrator explains that the children help their parents in the fields. Traditional, folk-like music with string instruments begins to play as the scene transitions to a moving vehicle, viewed from the side window, driving through a village with modest homes and children running alongside. The perspective shifts to the interior of the vehicle, showing a woman in a white shirt sitting in the back seat, holding a water bottle, as the vehicle travels along a dusty road with dry grasslands and distant hills under a golden sunset. The narrator's voice is heard reflecting on the experience, stating, "Back in the car, we feel strange. It hasn't felt right for us white, female journalists from Europe to examine people's lives here." A breathtaking view of the sun rising or setting over a vast, arid landscape, casting warm light across the terrain, is then shown. The scene returns to the vehicle’s interior, showing the driver and front passenger from behind, with a small black figurine hanging from the rearview mirror and a phone mounted on the dashboard. The narrator continues, "But we also believe it's important to report on something few people are aware of. We drive to another mine. But along the way, a problem presents itself." She explains that despite a colleague being back in the capital with COVID, the Ministry of Communications hasn't let up, keeping them calling, so they've come up with an excuse. The camera then focuses on the woman in the blue sweatshirt sitting in the front passenger seat, who turns to speak directly to the camera with a warm, expressive smile, gesturing as she talks, first in German, then translating: "I'm now writing to the woman from the Ministry of Communications to say that we're leaving on Sunday because we had too much trouble at the start of our trip and our editors won't give us any more money and that we have to go back to Germany." The vehicle continues along a rural road bordered by dry fields and sparse trees, bathed in the golden hue of the late afternoon or early evening sun.

As the camera shifts to the Man in blue and yellow jacket, identified by the narrator as Jimmy, driving along a dusty rural road, folk-style background music begins. The arid landscape outside, with sparse grass and distant hills, is visible as the vehicle passes a motorbike rider. The narrator explains there's "no cell phone reception at the mines" and introduces Jimmy as their guide, stating, "we're always either announced or accompanied by someone. This time, it's Jimmy." The scene then cuts to a rugged, sun-baked mining quarry where a Group of local workers, including the Man in orange sleeveless shirt, are laboring. The narrator describes Jimmy as "a sort of foreman at the mine," hired to supervise the work. The distinct sounds of pickaxes and shovels striking rock and scraping earth fill the audio as the men break and collect Large white crystals. Jimmy is seen walking down a steep path into the mine, while others carry heavy sacks up the slope. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a young man, Anjanaya Sanjaja, who introduces himself as 21 years old and having started mining at 17. He is visibly exhausted, covered in sweat and dust, wearing a dark sleeveless shirt with orange trim, and speaks with a strained, weary voice, gesturing as he explains the harsh conditions. Throughout the segment, the constant sound of a pickaxe striking earth and general digging noises underscore the manual labor. A female narrator interjects, stating that all workers claim to be over 18, and later reveals that the sun-baked, rocky quarry, with its steep, layered rock face, had collapsed a few months prior, requiring workers to dig the hole again. Anjanaya confirms that no one was hurt in that specific collapse as it happened when nobody was working, but later admits to having seen someone get hurt when a tunnel collapsed, though he gives a hesitant 'Mm' when asked if the person died. The camera pans up to emphasize the quarry's scale and danger, then shows a pickaxe striking the earth, breaking apart loose rock. A wider view reveals a boy in an orange shirt digging with a small tool, another boy in a yellow shirt climbing a narrow trench, and a boy in a green shirt carrying a large sack on his head across the uneven terrain. A man in a black and yellow high-visibility jacket and cap, identified by the narrator as Jimmy, the foreman, is seen inspecting the work, claiming it's rare for laborers to get hurt. Anjanaya is then shown crouching, examining a large, rough white crystal, his hands covered in dust, in the arid environment with sparse vegetation and bright sunlight, which casts harsh shadows. The narrator concludes by expressing skepticism about the foreman's claim, noting that almost everyone seen in the mine appears to be engaged in dangerous work.

The camera follows him as he walks through the rocky terrain, balancing the crystal on his head, his body covered in sweat and dust. The scene shifts to show him and another young man, wearing a faded green t-shirt, standing against a reddish rock face, both appearing weary and dirty. The narrator questions the laborers about their working conditions, noting their forced answers, while a worker explains, "We are always careful. We recognize when it is dangerous. Then we don't go down." When asked if they are worried, another worker admits, "Well, we are worried. But this is how we earn money. So we have to do it." The camera then pans to a wider view of the harsh, arid quarry, where several workers, including a man in a yellow shirt and another in a dark shirt, are using pickaxes and shovels to break apart rock and collect crystals, accompanied by the distinct sounds of pickaxes hitting rock. A makeshift shelter made of branches and thatch is visible, under which workers are sorting or resting. The narrator highlights that the work is better suited for machines, but permits are too expensive and no longer issued. The young man in the green shirt is later seen carrying a large sack on his head, walking carefully through the uneven, rocky ground, his bare feet visible, as the narrator asks how heavy the stones are that they have to carry. The environment, with its dry grass, scattered rocks, and steep, layered rock walls under bright sunlight, emphasizes the physical labor and difficult conditions of the mining work.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a large, rough, translucent white crystal, which a male narrator explains can weigh up to 160 kilos. The scene transitions to a dry, rocky quarry where a group of young men and boys, including those underage, labor under a thatched shelter, using basic tools like pickaxes and shovels to extract crystals. A female narrator highlights the harsh reality, noting how these individuals work without protective clothing or fixed hours, sometimes for a mere 10 cents per kilo, to feed their families. A boy carries a sack on his head, and another walks barefoot, underscoring the physical toll. The camera pans across a dry, grassy landscape before showing a moving vehicle's side mirror reflecting a dusty road, as a woman with blonde hair looks out thoughtfully. The narrator reflects on the absurdity of the 'hype around the stones' given these conditions, as they travel to Onsi B, a hub for rose quartz exporters. The journey leads to a bustling town street with tuk-tuks and shops, then to a red van parked near a warehouse with stacked white sacks. Inside a covered storage area, the 'Man in blue jacket', identified by the narrator as Fidi, stands beside a pile of large pinkish-white crystals. Fidi, speaking in English with an accent, explains to a woman in a white shirt that they 'make export and we collect,' buying 'many stone, variety of stone' when orders come in. He gestures towards the rough, jagged crystals, stating they have 'around 27 ton' of stone in his depot, which the narrator confirms.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a dimly lit, covered storage area, showcasing large heaps of rough, pinkish-white rose quartz crystals under a corrugated metal roof supported by wooden beams. A man, identified by the narrator as Fidy, wearing a blue jacket with a gray hood, stands beside the crystals, gesturing as he speaks to a woman in a light blue button-up shirt, who holds a notepad and pen, listening intently. Fidy explains that the crystals are for one container, specifically for India, and they load it on Saturday. The narrator adds that Fidy has specialized in rose quartz since 2010 and shows them the containers he ships monthly. Fidy then points to a container, stating it's full with "27 ton inside," and describes the sealing process. The woman asks if another shipment is also for China, to which Fidy clarifies it's for India. She then asks, "Who buys the most stones from you?" Fidy responds that Chinese and Indian customers are his primary buyers, noting he has no customers from Europe or the USA. The narrator further explains that Fidy finds it easy to get large quantities of rose quartz quickly, with his miners taking about one month to fill one container, before asking about the evolution of demand. Throughout, Fidy shows images of the crystals and possibly a mining video on his smartphone, which the camera zooms in on, revealing a red-lit storage area, connecting the raw crystals to their origin. The scene emphasizes the raw state of the crystals and the manual labor involved.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man in a blue jacket with a gray hood, standing inside a dimly lit, rustic, wooden-covered storage area filled with large pinkish-white crystals. He speaks animatedly, gesturing with his hands, explaining in a non-native English accent that in 2015 nobody wanted to buy rose quartz, but now the market is up, though its future is uncertain as it depends on the market in China's big cities. The camera pulls back to reveal a woman in a light blue button-up shirt and dark pants standing opposite him, listening intently with a notepad and pen. She interjects, stating that the stones rarely go directly to Europe, usually heading to China or India for processing. She then directly asks the man if the companies selling these processed stones in Germany know or can know their origin. The man, occasionally looking around the storage area, responds with certainty that he's sure they don't know, speculating that German traders buy in bulk from China, and Chinese sellers likely wouldn't disclose if the stones were from Madagascar. Their serious and informative discussion, set against the raw, industrial backdrop, highlights the complexities of the crystal trade.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a man in a blue 'NORTHLAND' jacket speaking inside a dimly lit wooden structure filled with pinkish-white crystals, likely a mining or storage site, as a male voiceover explains that local suppliers "would be afraid that big customers from Germany would come here directly," and that "they don't know." The scene transitions to a woman in a light blue button-up shirt, holding a notepad and pen, engaging with the man and another person, suggesting a journalistic inquiry. A distinct, slightly melancholic female voiceover, likely belonging to the woman with the notepad, then begins, stating, "Our research in Madagascar has come to an end. We've learned a lot about the mines and the working conditions, and about how important China is as a trading partner." They are then seen walking outside towards a vehicle under soft golden-hour lighting. The female voiceover continues, mentioning, "Again and again, we were told about the many Chinese buyers. We didn't manage to talk to one in Madagascar, but official figures confirm what we were told." The scene shifts to an airplane interior, showing a woman with long blonde hair looking out at the wing and clouds, then a patchwork landscape below, accompanied by the sound of an airplane, as the female voiceover explains, "About half of the precious and semi-precious stones from Madagascar are exported to China. Germany is ranked fourth among export destinations with much lower trade volume." This aligns with a bar graph displaying market values of crystal exports by country (China, USA, France, Germany, Sri Lanka) from the UN Comtrade Database 2020. The female voiceover concludes, "Back in Hamburg, we decide to try a different approach to get in touch with Chinese suppliers," as the final shot reveals delicate crystal jewelry with rose quartz and gold accents on a white surface, showcasing the end product.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in a modern, brightly lit office where a young man, identified by a female narrator as her colleague Max, works at a desk with a laptop and an iMac. The iMac screen displays the pink and white 'SOA Organics' website, a fake startup created by Max to sell rose quartz products, featuring 'fair and certified skin-care products' and 'Madagascan rose quartz' with German text. A woman with short brown hair, wearing a dark gray top, joins Max, and they discuss the project. Max, speaking with a German accent, questions the need for a fake website, while the woman, also with a German accent, explains their goal is to investigate the processing step in China, as they already know about traders in Germany and extraction in Madagascar. She gestures toward several polished rose quartz crystals laid out on the desk, which are central to their investigation. The female narrator explains that under the guise of their fake company, they visit a Chinese trading platform, likely Alibaba, to find suppliers for 10,000 rose quartz facial rollers. The camera cuts to close-ups of the website and then to the laptop screen, showing the Alibaba messaging interface with a '35' notification badge. As the narrator states their inbox "explodes" with responses, a distinct notification sound is heard. Max is seen sending a message in English to a supplier, asking for information about mining conditions and certificates for the rose quartz, as the narrator emphasizes their intent to inquire about working conditions in the mines. Throughout the segment, a light, investigative background music plays, underscoring the professional yet ethically probing atmosphere of the office.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens on a laptop screen displaying an Alibaba messaging interface, where 'SOA Organics' (a female voice) is seen and heard inquiring about the sourcing of rose quartz, specifically asking for certificates or details on mining conditions. A supplier (a male voice) responds evasively, stating that such details are confidential to prevent revealing trade secrets. SOA presses further, asking about certificates for the quality of the rollers, to which the supplier again reiterates confidentiality. The interface then shifts to another conversation where SOA (female voice) asks about working conditions in the mines, which is met with a blunt "No, we don't" from a different male supplier voice. A third male supplier voice similarly states they have no information regarding their stones. The female voice of SOA then provides context, mentioning they also emailed Chinese suppliers. The final visual shows an email response, where a male voice acknowledges concerns about legal mining and child labor but states they have no information on these matters, though they offer a video of the mining process. Throughout these digital exchanges, the 'My Alibaba' header, 'Local Time: 00:32', and a '35' notification badge remain visible, underscoring the transactional nature of these inquiries which consistently meet with a lack of transparency from suppliers.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a laptop screen displaying an email from a supplier to 'SOA Organics,' where a calm male voice acknowledges concerns about child labor and the legality of rose quartz mining, offering only a video of the mining as evidence. The screen then shows a video clip of a man in a blue shirt loading large, rough white crystals into a sack beside a truck in a dry, rocky environment. A female narrator, with a slightly skeptical tone, immediately critiques the video, stating it "doesn't even show the work in the mines." The scene cuts to this same woman, with short brown hair, wearing a black blazer, sitting at a desk in a modern office, intently watching the video on her computer, her expression shifting from neutral to concerned. She continues to narrate, explaining their undercover research where Chinese suppliers were forthcoming with mock photos but unhelpful regarding actual working conditions in the mines. As upbeat, investigative-style background music begins, the scene transitions to a car driving through a scenic rural landscape, then shows the woman, now in a floral blouse and carrying a backpack, walking through a bustling outdoor gemstone market. She browses various stalls filled with polished crystals, including large pink rose quartz spheres and other gemstones, with handwritten price tags like 'Quartz Ros Coeur 25€/kg' and '250€' visible. Amidst the lively market sounds and chatter, a German-speaking voice is briefly heard. The narrator points out a label saying "Madagascar" and observes that "here again, there's a lot of rose quartz," reflecting the journey from ethical sourcing concerns to the physical marketplace where these materials are sold.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of pale pink rose quartz crystals, tagged with prices like '16€' and '25€', set against an upbeat, light electronic music track. The camera then pans across a bustling mineral market stall under a white tent, revealing an array of raw and polished stones, with handwritten signs for 'Eudialyte Norway 13.35 kg' and 'Turmalin Madagascar'. A female narrator explains that many retailers are aware of the risks miners face and their low earnings, specifically mentioning a wholesaler from Germany, while market chatter is audible. The scene shifts to an outdoor interview with a man with a white beard and short gray hair, wearing a black t-shirt and a 'Mineral & Gem' lanyard, speaking into an NDR microphone. This man, identified as the German wholesaler, states in German (with an English voiceover) that they cannot pay the workers, only the owner, and that while this cannot be changed, direct payment to the mine would be better than a wholesaler pocketing it. He further explains that for customers, 80% care about price and quality, with very few asking about ethical sourcing, which he considers their responsibility. The camera then cuts to a display of large, polished stone bowls and vases before transitioning to another tent where a man in a beige shirt and cap, also with a 'Mineral & Gem' lanyard, is seated, writing on a notepad while being interviewed with an NDR microphone. The narrator concludes that whether retailers live up to their responsibility is doubtful, according to a conversation with this 'another salesman', who is then asked about working conditions in the mines for the miners.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a man in a beige shirt and cap, wearing a 'MINERAL GEM' lanyard, seated at a market stall inside a white tent. He is seen writing on a notepad while being interviewed by a reporter holding a blue NDR microphone. The man, speaking with a distinct non-native English accent, discusses efforts to improve the working conditions of his own workers and suppliers by paying 'quite decent prices,' acknowledging the challenges of what happens 'on the mine.' Behind him, a woman with dark hair in a patterned top arranges mineral specimens on a glass shelf. The camera zooms in on the man's face as he speaks, capturing his expressive gestures and focused demeanor. He continues, noting that 'even on this small area, there is seven, at least seven, maybe eight exhibitors who are carrying Madagascan stones,' and that 'no one is very, very involved in what's happened on the mine.' A wider shot reveals the market stall filled with polished stones, wooden crates, and large stone bowls. As light, slightly mysterious background music begins, the off-screen reporter's female voice asks about customer inquiries regarding 'working conditions or child labor.' The man, his face showing engagement, responds controversially, stating he can 'take any photos and delete the one with the kids are on,' and that he will 'show you what you want to see and don't show you what you don't want to see.' The scene cuts to a close-up of small, polished reddish-brown stones with price tags on a colorful tablecloth, and then to a glass display case containing triangular pink crystals and a large, rough orange mineral, both with handwritten price tags, as the reporter's voice begins another question.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of polished pink rose quartz crystals, transitioning to a bustling outdoor mineral and gem fair under white tents, where various polished stones and raw minerals are displayed. An English female narrator's voice begins, explaining that the German Supply Chain Act, in effect since January 2023, aims to protect the rights of people producing goods for the German market. As she speaks, a woman with red hair, wearing a white face mask, is seen on a train, typing on a laptop displaying German text about mining conditions and certificates for rose quartz, visually connecting to the narrator's topic. The narrator questions if the law holds up, noting it was weakened and now only applies to larger companies. The train arrives at Schwäbisch Gmünd station. The narrator then states they are in Schwäbisch Gmünd to discuss this with Norbert Bartler. Visually, the woman, now with short brown hair, a white t-shirt, and a backpack, walks through a sunny suburban neighborhood towards a house with a blue door. In the garden, she meets an older man in a light blue suit, to whom she hands a small black microphone. She greets him in German, saying "Hallo Herr Bartler," and introduces herself as "Nadja Mützkat," to which he responds in German. A camera on a tripod is set up to capture their interview in the sunlit garden. The narrator provides background, stating Bartler played a key role in the Supply Chain Act for the CDU party as a state secretary, while the man and woman continue their conversation in German, with the woman responding "Mhm."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of an older man in a light blue suit and yellow-striped tie, seated outdoors on a white wooden bench in a sunlit garden for an interview. He speaks expressively, gesturing with his hands, a small black lavalier microphone clipped to his lapel. He states, in German with an English translation, that it "was one of the most difficult legislative processes I encountered in my entire parliamentary career," identifying Peter Altmaier and the Economic Affairs Committee as "fiercest opponent[s]" who "showed fierce opposition." The camera alternates between close-ups of his face and over-the-shoulder shots showing the back of a woman interviewer, wearing a white top, gold hoop earrings, and a gold necklace, with her hair tied back in a bun. The setting is lush with green foliage and bright natural lighting. The interviewer's voice, acting as a narrator, explains, "Amongst other stipulations, we're told Altmaier objected to the following." The man then poses questions like, "How deep should you go? Should the entire supply chain be covered by the law or not?" adding that "The business community has always said only to first supply level. And no further." A wider shot reveals the full interview setup, with the man on one bench, the woman on another, and a small wooden table between them holding glasses of water and a plate. A professional camera on a tripod with a large lens and foam-covered microphone captures the scene. The woman listens attentively, occasionally responding, and her voice continues to narrate, stating, "We contacted the former Minister for Economic Affairs, Peter Altmaier, who responded that the law could only be passed because of this compromise." She further explains, "The new law obliges German companies to ensure human rights compliance, but with indirect suppliers only after a complaint has been filed." She then adds, in German and translated, "We asked big retailers. But they usually didn't answer or were very evasive." The scene remains calm and professional, with a 'DW' logo visible, suggesting a formal outdoor interview.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of the Woman with short brown hair, seated on a white bench in a sunlit garden, speaking expressively with hand gestures. Wearing a white top, gold hoop earrings, and a layered gold necklace, with her hair in a bun and a lavalier microphone clipped to her shirt, she states in a clear, articulate voice, "Then we pretended to be a fake startup and asked the suppliers in China." She continues, explaining that suppliers were willing to disclose origins but not working conditions, always responding, "we can't say." She then poses a critical question in German, translated as, "But as a German company, that would be all I'd need under the new supply chain law, right? I wouldn't have to do anything, right?" The camera cuts to the Man in blue suit, seated across from her, dressed in a light blue suit, white shirt, and a yellow and blue striped tie, also with a lavalier microphone. He listens intently, then responds with a calm, authoritative voice, affirming "Yes," and elaborating, "Then you would have fulfilled your responsibility. You asked. And if there are no suspicions, if there are no complaints or other information, then that's all that's legally required." The woman, still gesturing, challenges this, asking, "But isn't that too little?" The man, gesturing as he speaks, acknowledges this as a "big point of contention," discussing the depth and binding nature of supply chain requirements, stating, "to what level of depth in the supply chain and how binding do you go?" The background remains lush green foliage with dappled sunlight, and the DW logo is visible throughout, framing this engaged discussion.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens in a sunlit garden where a man in a light blue suit and yellow-striped tie, identified as the 'Man in blue suit', engages in conversation with a woman in a white t-shirt and jeans, the 'Woman with short brown hair', who holds a small camera and wears a lavalier microphone. A male voice, speaking in German then translated to English, discusses future binding, followed by a female voice stating that a stricter law couldn't have been passed. The woman thanks the man for the interview, to which the man's voice replies, "It was a pleasure. I'm still working on this." As the man enters a white house, the woman remains outside, then walks down a quiet suburban street, speaking directly to the camera. Her female voice narrates, explaining that "Bartler is happy to have gotten the law passed at all," but emphasizes that "the law will not change anything for the people we saw in Madagascar, for the people in the mines. And that's a bitter pill to swallow." She continues, explaining how German companies might use this "supply chain law" as an excuse, but only up to a certain level, which is "China, not Madagascar." Upbeat, slightly melancholic instrumental music begins as the scene shifts to a dry, rocky quarry. The woman's voice then asks, "So, what have we learned from our research?" and continues to narrate over visuals of young men in the quarry – one in a red and white jacket, another in a gray polo shirt, and a third in a brown sleeveless 'BULLS' jersey – stating, "We met people who pay a high price for products whose benefits don't justify that price. No one takes responsibility for the people at the beginning of the..." The bright, natural lighting consistently highlights the contrast between the serene suburban setting and the arid, labor-intensive quarry.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a man in a beige shirt and cap, wearing a 'MINERAL GEM' lanyard and a red bracelet, seated at a market stall under a white tent, writing on a notepad while being interviewed with an NDR microphone. A female narrator begins, stating, "They're not heard or even seen," a comment underscored by continuous, slightly melancholic background music. The narration continues, "That's almost always the story in globalization," as the scene transitions to a young man with curly black hair and a mustache, wearing a brown zip-up jacket, smiling indoors amidst warm lighting and plants. The visual then shifts to a young man in a dusty beige t-shirt in a dry, rocky quarry, surrounded by other workers carrying sacks and tools under bright sunlight, while the narrator concludes, "And Germany's Supply Chain Act won't change that." An older man in a light blue suit, white shirt, and yellow-and-blue striped tie is subsequently seen speaking into a lavalier microphone in a sunlit garden with trees. The final sequence shows a group of local workers in the dry, sunlit quarry, some sitting on a wooden cart, others standing amidst large white crystals and sacks. On-screen text credits the film to Nadja Mitzkat, Zita Zengerling, and Hantarinoro Holifeno, followed by camera, video editors, graphics, narrator, commissioning editor Kathrin Becker, NDR, and Deutsche Welle 2023. The visuals starkly contrast the commercial market and natural garden settings with the harsh, arid mining landscape, emphasizing the labor-intensive aspects of the crystal trade, all while the background music persists.
